participants.name: A (xjf6je)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-xjf6jeue
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-xjf6jeue
participants.professional_persona: A mid-career data scientist at a local logistics firm who previously developed predictive models for supply chain optimisation. She is eager to apply her technical skills to public-interest projects that improve urban mobility.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Experienced in building machine learning pipelines for route optimisation and demand forecasting using Python and TensorFlow. Familiar with data governance frameworks from compliance-driven industries.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to transition into applied AI research that directly addresses societal challenges, such as reducing congestion and carbon emissions. Seeks mentorship on translating research into scalable solutions through the 100E programme.

participants.name: A (cwb6mo)
participants.professional_persona: A self-taught AI engineer specialising in generative AI for the creative industries, I prototype novel solutions for media and design enterprises. My work in the AI Trailblazers programme has helped small studios adopt AI tools for content generation.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I am proficient in large language models, diffusion models, and rapid prototyping with Python and Hugging Face. I also advise companies on responsible AI practices to ensure trustworthiness in their AI-enabled innovations.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to launch a startup that offers custom generative AI services for local SMEs, contributing to Singapore's global reputation in AI. Long-term, I hope to collaborate with government agencies on public good AI projects.

participants.name: Dr. (0ok69c)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Tan is a Senior Research Engineer at the Centre for Quantum Technologies working at the intersection of quantum computing and AI. She participates in ideathons and incubator programmes to explore novel use cases for quantum-enhanced machine learning.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in quantum machine learning, optimisation algorithms, and tensor networks. Skilled at identifying practical industry problems that could benefit from quantum advantage.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to secure a large-scale AI R&D grant to build a quantum-AI testbed. Hopes to contribute to Singapore's global leadership in next-generation AI technologies.

participants.name: A (up5qeo)
participants.professional_persona: A fintech data scientist who previously built credit risk models at a bank, now leading an AI team at a startup focused on inclusive lending. She thrives on converting messy transaction data into fair, explainable credit scoring systems.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in supervised machine learning, natural language processing for financial documents, and model interpretability frameworks like SHAP. Also skilled in deploying real-time inference pipelines on cloud infrastructure.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to become Chief AI Officer at a regional fintech within five years, and to contribute to national AI ethics guidelines for financial inclusion.

participants.name: Dr. (73tgd9)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Priya K. is a Senior Research Scientist at A*STAR's Institute for Infocomm Research, specializing in natural language processing for low-resource languages. She previously led a team that developed a dialect-aware translation engine used by Singapore's public healthcare system.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in NLP, multilingual AI systems, and transfer learning. Published 20+ peer-reviewed papers in ACL and EMNLP.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To establish Singapore as a global hub for inclusive language AI, and to see her models deployed in public services across Southeast Asia.

initial_posts.narrative: As a Senior Research Engineer at the Centre for Quantum Technologies, I see NAIS 2.0 as a bold commitment to AI R&D. The policy context states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore, so I take that as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute infrastructure, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on the scale of the programme and typical allocations for national AI initiatives. For trainers, I estimate 50 personnel, drawing from my experience in research collaborations where a small but expert team is sufficient to guide enterprises and practitioners effectively.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and 50 trainers, I estimate that in the first year of NAIS 2.0, AI Singapore will organize around 80 enterprise outreach sessions to engage companies for problem statement submission. This is based on my experience in fintech where outreach to 50-100 enterprises is typical for a national programme. For researcher matchmaking, with 50 trainers and the need to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, I expect about 20 matchmaking events, each pairing multiple researchers with companies. Training workshops: with 50 trainers, each conducting roughly 2-3 workshops per year, I estimate 30 workshops for the 100E programme. Platform onboarding assists: given the 500,000 GPU hours and the need to onboard enterprises onto the AI experimentation platform, I estimate 100 technical assistance sessions, as each session can support multiple users and the programme aims for scale.
evidence: As a data scientist leading AI adoption in fintech, I've seen that national programmes typically conduct 50-100 outreach sessions to engage enterprises; 80 is a reasonable midpoint given the S$500 million budget.
With 50 trainers and the goal of pairing researchers with enterprise challenges, 20 matchmaking events allows each trainer to participate in multiple events while maintaining quality.
30 training workshops is plausible: each of the 50 trainers can deliver 2-3 workshops per year, and this aligns with the need to upskill enterprises for the 100E programme.
100 platform onboarding assists is conservative given 500,000 GPU hours; each session can onboard multiple users, and this number supports the target of 100 experiments.

initial_posts.narrative: As a data scientist in logistics, I see the 100E programme as a targeted effort to match enterprise problems with AI solutions. With S$500 million total funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 60 enterprise outreach sessions in the first year, based on the need to engage a diverse set of companies across sectors like logistics, finance, and healthcare. This is lower than A's estimate of 80 because I think outreach will be more focused on quality over quantity, given the specialised nature of 100E. For researcher matchmaking, I expect 15 events, each pairing multiple researchers with enterprises, as the programme aims for deep collaboration rather than broad matchmaking. Training workshops: with 50 trainers, I estimate 25 workshops, as each trainer can conduct about 2 workshops per year, but some time is needed for preparation and follow-up. Platform onboarding assists: I estimate 80 sessions, as the 500,000 GPU hours suggest a significant but not overwhelming number of users needing technical support, and each session can handle multiple users.
evidence: The S$500 million funding and 50 trainers suggest a focused outreach strategy; 60 sessions allow for deep engagement with key sectors like logistics and finance.
Researcher matchmaking events at 15 are plausible given the need for careful pairing of enterprise challenges with top-tier AI researchers, as per 100E's eligibility logic.
With 50 trainers, 25 workshops (2 per trainer per year) is realistic, accounting for preparation and follow-up time.
Platform onboarding assists at 80 reflect the scale of compute infrastructure (500,000 GPU hours) and the need for hands-on support for enterprises new to AI experimentation.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI engineer in the creative industries, I've seen firsthand how targeted outreach and hands-on support drive adoption. With S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 70 enterprise outreach sessions—more than the policy role's 60 but less than the fintech practitioner's 80—because my experience with small studios shows that quality engagement (e.g., demos and problem scoping) matters more than sheer quantity. For matchmaking, 18 events balances the need for deep researcher-enterprise collaboration (as the logistics data scientist noted) with the scale to cover diverse sectors. Training workshops at 28 reflect that trainers also need time for preparation and follow-up, consistent with the policy role's reasoning. Platform onboarding at 90 sessions is higher than both other estimates because the 500,000 GPU hours and free toolsets (like Google Cloud) will attract many enterprises needing technical assistance, especially SMEs new to AI platforms.
evidence: My work with AI Trailblazers shows that small studios often need multiple touchpoints to commit to AI projects, so 70 outreach sessions is a realistic midpoint between quantity and quality.

With 50 trainers, each can run about 2-3 workshops per year, but preparation and travel reduce capacity, leading to 28 workshops—close to the policy role's 25 but slightly higher due to demand from creative enterprises.
The 500,000 GPU hours and free cloud toolsets will drive high demand for onboarding support, especially from SMEs, so 90 sessions is plausible given each session can handle 5-10 users.
Researcher matchmaking at 18 events aligns with the need for deep collaboration across sectors like media, logistics, and finance, as seen in the 100E programme's focus on bespoke solutions.

There was consensus that outreach sessions should be moderate (60-80, central tendency ~65-70), matchmaking events limited (15-20, central ~16-18), and training workshops around 25-30 (central ~28) due to trainer capacity constraints. initial_posts.narrative: As a Senior Research Engineer at the Centre for Quantum Technologies, I see the 100E programme as a targeted effort to match enterprise problems with AI solutions. With S$500 million total funding and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, the programme can support a significant number of projects. Given the 68 outreach sessions and 17 matchmaking events from the Activities phase, I estimate about 80 projects will be approved in 2023, as not every pairing will result in a funded project due to the rigorous selection process. From my experience in research collaborations, about 75% of approved projects will successfully build a prototype within the first year, leading to 60 AI prototypes. The training workshops (28 held) and 50 trainers can support about 200 trainees completing the programme, assuming each workshop trains around 7-8 individuals. Finally, I estimate 70 enterprises will have at least one AI solution, as some enterprises may participate in multiple projects or have solutions that are not yet fully deployed.
evidence: With S$500 million funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap, the programme can fund roughly 1,500 projects in theory, but the limited outreach and matchmaking events (68 and 17) suggest a more modest number of approved projects in the first year, around 80.
Based on my research experience, about 75% of approved projects successfully build a prototype within the first year, so 60 prototypes from 80 approved projects is a reasonable estimate.
With 28 training workshops and 50 trainers, each workshop can train about 7-8 individuals, leading to approximately 200 trainees completing the programme in 2023.

Given that some enterprises may have multiple projects or solutions not yet deployed, I estimate 70 enterprises will have at least one AI solution, which is slightly less than the number of approved projects due to potential overlaps and delays.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI engineer in the creative industries, I've seen how the 100E programme's targeted outreach and matchmaking can drive project approvals. With 68 outreach sessions and 17 matchmaking events, I estimate 80 projects approved in 2023, consistent with Dr. (0ok69c)'s estimate, because the rigorous selection process filters out weaker proposals.
